message.content:
Explore the picorv32 repository at /home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/YosysHQ-picorv32 with "medium" breadth. I need a concise structural summary (not full content) of these files for designing a learning handbook. For each file, report: its purpose, the main functions/modules/symbols it defines, and 2-4 key learning points. Be concise.

Files to summarize:
1. firmware/start.S  (startup code, interrupt handler wrapper, register save/restore)
2. firmware/custom_ops.S  (the custom IRQ instruction assembler macros: getq/setq/retirq/maskirq/waitirq/timer)
3. firmware/irq.c  (the C interrupt handler implementation)
4. firmware/firmware.h, firmware/hello.c, firmware/print.c, firmware/sections.lds, firmware/makehex.py
5. firmware/multest.c and firmware/sieve.c and firmware/stats.c (what they test)
6. picosoc/README.md  (the example SoC overview, memory map, how it boots from SPI flash)
7. picosoc/picosoc.v  (top-level SoC module: what modules it instantiates — picorv32, spimemio, simpleuart, etc., and key signals)
8. picosoc/spimemio.v  (SPI flash memory interface controller — briefly)
9. picosoc/simpleuart.v  (the UART — briefly)
10. picosoc/firmware.c and picosoc/start.s (briefly)
11. picosoc/hx8kdemo.v and picosoc/icebreaker.v (the two FPGA board wrappers — briefly)
12. testbench.cc (the Verilator C++ testbench)
13. testbench.v (top of file: what does the picorv32_wrapper module do, what memory model does it use, what +args does it support)
14. testbench_wb.v (the Wishbone testbench — briefly)
15. showtrace.py (what it does)
16. scripts/vivado/ (list files and the purpose of the Makefile there — area/timing eval)
17. scripts/smtbmc/ (formal verification scripts)
18. scripts/cxxdemo/ (C++ demo — what files, purpose)
19. scripts/icestorm/ and scripts/yosys/ (briefly)
20. picorv32.core and picosoc/*.core files (FuseSoC core files — what they declare)

Report findings as a structured list grouped by file. Keep each file's summary to a few lines. Do not paste large code blocks; describe structure and key symbols only.

1	
2	PicoSoC - A simple example SoC using PicoRV32
3	=============================================
4	
5	![](overview.svg)
6	
7	This is a simple PicoRV32 example design that can run code directly from an SPI
8	flash chip. It can be used as a turn-key solution for simple control tasks in
9	ASIC and FPGA designs.
10	
11	An example implementation targeting the Lattice iCE40-HX8K Breakout Board is
12	included.
13	
14	The flash is mapped to the memory regions starting at 0x00000000 and
15	0x01000000, with the SRAM overlayed for the mapping at 0x00000000. The SRAM
16	is just a small scratchpad memory (default 256 words, i.e. 1 kB).
17	
18	The reset vector is set to 0x00100000, i.e. at 1MB into in the flash memory.
19	
20	See the included demo firmware and linker script for how to build a firmware
21	image for this system.
22	
23	Run `make hx8ksim` or `make icebsim` to run the test bench (and create `testbench.vcd`).
24	
25	Run `make hx8kprog` to build the configuration bit-stream and firmware images
26	and upload them to a connected iCE40-HX8K Breakout Board.
27	
28	Run `make icebprog` to build the configuration bit-stream and firmware images
29	and upload them to a connected iCEBreaker Board.
30	
31	| File                                | Description                                                     |
32	| ----------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
33	| [picosoc.v](picosoc.v)              | Top-level PicoSoC Verilog module                                |
34	| [spimemio.v](spimemio.v)            | Memory controller that interfaces to external SPI flash         |
35	| [simpleuart.v](simpleuart.v)        | Simple UART core connected directly to SoC TX/RX lines          |
36	| [start.s](start.s)                  | Assembler source for firmware.hex/firmware.bin                  |
37	| [firmware.c](firmware.c)            | C source for firmware.hex/firmware.bin                          |
38	| [sections.lds](sections.lds)        | Linker script for firmware.hex/firmware.bin                     |
39	| [hx8kdemo.v](hx8kdemo.v)            | FPGA-based example implementation on iCE40-HX8K Breakout Board  |
40	| [hx8kdemo.pcf](hx8kdemo.pcf)        | Pin constraints for implementation on iCE40-HX8K Breakout Board |
41	| [hx8kdemo\_tb.v](hx8kdemo_tb.v)     | Testbench for implementation on iCE40-HX8K Breakout Board       |
42	| [icebreaker.v](icebreaker.v)        | FPGA-based example implementation on iCEBreaker Board           |
43	| [icebreaker.pcf](icebreaker.pcf)    | Pin constraints for implementation on iCEBreaker Board          |
44	| [icebreaker\_tb.v](icebreaker_tb.v) | Testbench for implementation on iCEBreaker Board                |
45	
46	### Memory map:
47	
48	| Address Range            | Description                             |
49	| ------------------------ | --------------------------------------- |
50	| 0x00000000 .. 0x00FFFFFF | Internal SRAM                           |
51	| 0x01000000 .. 0x01FFFFFF | External Serial Flash                   |
52	| 0x02000000 .. 0x02000003 | SPI Flash Controller Config Register    |
53	| 0x02000004 .. 0x02000007 | UART Clock Divider Register             |
54	| 0x02000008 .. 0x0200000B | UART Send/Recv Data Register            |
55	| 0x03000000 .. 0xFFFFFFFF | Memory mapped user peripherals          |
56	
57	Reading from the addresses in the internal SRAM region beyond the end of the
58	physical SRAM will read from the corresponding addresses in serial flash.
59	
60	Reading from the UART Send/Recv Data Register will return the last received
61	byte, or -1 (all 32 bits set) when the receive buffer is empty.
62	
63	The UART Clock Divider Register must be set to the system clock frequency
64	divided by the baud rate.
65	
66	The example design (hx8kdemo.v) has the 8 LEDs on the iCE40-HX8K Breakout Board
67	mapped to the low byte of the 32 bit word at address 0x03000000.
68	
69	### SPI Flash Controller Config Register:
70	
71	| Bit(s) | Description                                               |
72	| -----: | --------------------------------------------------------- |
73	|     31 | MEMIO Enable (reset=1, set to 0 to bit bang SPI commands) |
74	|  30:23 | Reserved (read 0)                                         |
75	|     22 | DDR Enable bit (reset=0)                                  |
76	|     21 | QSPI Enable bit (reset=0)                                 |
77	|     20 | CRM Enable bit (reset=0)                                  |
78	|  19:16 | Read latency (dummy) cycles (reset=8)                     |
79	|  15:12 | Reserved (read 0)                                         |
80	|   11:8 | IO Output enable bits in bit bang mode                    |
81	|    7:6 | Reserved (read 0)                                         |
82	|      5 | Chip select (CS) line in bit bang mode                    |
83	|      4 | Serial clock line in bit bang mode                        |
84	|    3:0 | IO data bits in bit bang mode                             |
85	
86	The following settings for CRM/DDR/QSPI modes are valid:
87	
88	| CRM | QSPI | DDR | Read Command Byte     | Mode Byte |
89	| :-: | :--: | :-: | :-------------------- | :-------: |
90	|   0 |    0 |   0 | 03h Read              | N/A       |
91	|   0 |    0 |   1 | BBh Dual I/O Read     | FFh       |
92	|   1 |    0 |   1 | BBh Dual I/O Read     | A5h       |
93	|   0 |    1 |   0 | EBh Quad I/O Read     | FFh       |
94	|   1 |    1 |   0 | EBh Quad I/O Read     | A5h       |
95	|   0 |    1 |   1 | EDh DDR Quad I/O Read | FFh       |
96	|   1 |    1 |   1 | EDh DDR Quad I/O Read | A5h       |
97	
98	The following plot visualizes the relative performance of the different configurations:
99	
100	![](performance.png)
101	
102	Consult the datasheet for your SPI flash to learn which configurations are supported
103	by the chip and what the maximum clock frequencies are for each configuration.
104	
105	For Quad I/O mode the QUAD flag in CR1V must be set before enabling Quad I/O in the
106	SPI master. Either set it by writing the corresponding bit in CR1NV once, or by writing
107	it from your device firmware at every bootup. (See `set_flash_qspi_flag()` in
108	`firmware.c` for an example for the latter.)
109	
110	Note that some changes to the Lattice iCE40-HX8K Breakout Board are required to support
111	the faster configurations: (1) The flash chip must be replaced with one that supports the
112	faster read commands and (2) the IO2 and IO3 pins on the flash chip must be connected to
113	the FPGA IO pins T9 and T8 (near the center of J3).

98	flashio_worker_begin:
99	# a0 ... data pointer
100	# a1 ... data length
101	# a2 ... optional WREN cmd (0 = disable)
102	
103	# address of SPI ctrl reg
104	li   t0, 0x02000000
105	
106	# Set CS high, IO0 is output
107	li   t1, 0x120
108	sh   t1, 0(t0)
109	
110	# Enable Manual SPI Ctrl
111	sb   zero, 3(t0)
112	
113	# Send optional WREN cmd
114	beqz a2, flashio_worker_L1
115	li   t5, 8
116	andi t2, a2, 0xff
117	flashio_worker_L4:
118	srli t4, t2, 7
119	sb   t4, 0(t0)
120	ori  t4, t4, 0x10
121	sb   t4, 0(t0)
122	slli t2, t2, 1
123	andi t2, t2, 0xff
124	addi t5, t5, -1
125	bnez t5, flashio_worker_L4
126	sb   t1, 0(t0)
127	
128	# SPI transfer
129	flashio_worker_L1:
130	beqz a1, flashio_worker_L3
131	li   t5, 8
132	lbu  t2, 0(a0)
133	flashio_worker_L2:
134	srli t4, t2, 7
135	sb   t4, 0(t0)
136	ori  t4, t4, 0x10
137	sb   t4, 0(t0)
138	lbu  t4, 0(t0)
139	andi t4, t4, 2
140	srli t4, t4, 1
141	slli t2, t2, 1
142	or   t2, t2, t4
143	andi t2, t2, 0xff
144	addi t5, t5, -1
145	bnez t5, flashio_worker_L2
146	sb   t2, 0(a0)
147	addi a0, a0, 1
148	addi a1, a1, -1
149	j    flashio_worker_L1
150	flashio_worker_L3:
151	
152	# Back to MEMIO mode
153	li   t1, 0x80
154	sb   t1, 3(t0)
155	
156	ret
157	
158	.balign 4
159	flashio_worker_end:

189	module wb_ram #(
190		parameter depth = 256,
191		parameter memfile = "",
192		parameter VERBOSE = 0
193	) (
194		input wb_clk_i,
195		input wb_rst_i,
196	
197		input [31:0] wb_adr_i,
198		input [31:0] wb_dat_i,
199		input [3:0] wb_sel_i,
200		input wb_we_i,
201		input wb_cyc_i,
202		input wb_stb_i,
203	
204		output reg wb_ack_o,
205		output reg [31:0] wb_dat_o,
206	
207		input mem_instr,
208		output reg tests_passed
209	);
210	
211		reg verbose;
212		initial verbose = $test$plusargs("verbose") || VERBOSE;
213	
214		initial tests_passed = 0;
215	
216		reg [31:0] adr_r;
217		wire valid = wb_cyc_i & wb_stb_i;
218	
219		always @(posedge wb_clk_i) begin
220			adr_r <= wb_adr_i;
221			// Ack generation
222			wb_ack_o <= valid & !wb_ack_o;
223			if (wb_rst_i)
224			begin
225				adr_r <= {32{1'b0}};
226				wb_ack_o <= 1'b0;
227			end
228		end
229	
230		wire ram_we = wb_we_i & valid & wb_ack_o;
231	
232		wire [31:0] waddr = adr_r[31:2];
233		wire [31:0] raddr = wb_adr_i[31:2];
234		wire [3:0] we = {4{ram_we}} & wb_sel_i;
235	
236		wire [$clog2(depth/4)-1:0] raddr2 = raddr[$clog2(depth/4)-1:0];
237		wire [$clog2(depth/4)-1:0] waddr2 = waddr[$clog2(depth/4)-1:0];
238	
239		reg [31:0] mem [0:depth/4-1] /* verilator public */;
240	
241		always @(posedge wb_clk_i) begin
242			if (ram_we) begin
243				if (verbose)
244					$display("WR: ADDR=%08x DATA=%08x STRB=%04b",
245						adr_r, wb_dat_i, we);
246	
247				if (adr_r[31:0] == 32'h1000_0000)
248					if (verbose) begin
249						if (32 <= wb_dat_i[7:0] && wb_dat_i[7:0] < 128)
250							$display("OUT: '%c'", wb_dat_i[7:0]);
251						else
252							$display("OUT: %3d", wb_dat_i[7:0]);
253					end else begin
254						$write("%c", wb_dat_i[7:0]);
255	`ifndef VERILATOR
256						$fflush();
257	`endif
258					end
259				else
260				if (adr_r[31:0] == 32'h2000_0000)
261					if (wb_dat_i[31:0] == 123456789)
262						tests_passed = 1;
263			end
264		end
265	
266		always @(posedge wb_clk_i) begin
267			if (waddr2 < 128 * 1024 / 4) begin
268				if (we[0])
269					mem[waddr2][7:0] <= wb_dat_i[7:0];
270	
271				if (we[1])
272					mem[waddr2][15:8] <= wb_dat_i[15:8];
273	
274				if (we[2])
275					mem[waddr2][23:16] <= wb_dat_i[23:16];
276	
277				if (we[3])
278					mem[waddr2][31:24] <= wb_dat_i[31:24];
279	
280			end
281	
282			if (valid & wb_ack_o & !ram_we)
283				if (verbose)
284					$display("RD: ADDR=%08x DATA=%08x%s", adr_r, mem[raddr2], mem_instr ? " INSN" : "");
285	
286			wb_dat_o <= mem[raddr2];
287		end
288	
289		initial begin
290			if (memfile != "")
291				$readmemh(memfile, mem);
292		end
293	endmodule

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	
3	import sys, re, subprocess
4	
5	trace_filename = sys.argv[1]
6	elf_filename = sys.argv[2]
7	
8	insns = dict()
9	
10	with subprocess.Popen(["riscv32-unknown-elf-objdump", "-d", elf_filename], stdout=subprocess.PIPE) as proc:
11	    while True:
12	        line = proc.stdout.readline().decode("ascii")
13	        if line == '': break
14	        match = re.match(r'^\s*([0-9a-f]+):\s+([0-9a-f]+)\s*(.*)', line)
15	        if match: insns[int(match.group(1), 16)] = (int(match.group(2), 16), match.group(3).replace("\t", " "))
16	
17	with open(trace_filename, "r") as f:
18	    pc = -1
19	    last_irq = False
20	    for line in f:
21	        raw_data = int(line.replace("x", "0"), 16)
22	        payload = raw_data & 0xffffffff
23	        irq_active = (raw_data & 0x800000000) != 0
24	        is_addr = (raw_data & 0x200000000) != 0
25	        is_branch = (raw_data & 0x100000000) != 0
26	        info = "%s %s%08x" % ("IRQ" if irq_active or last_irq else "   ",
27	                ">" if is_branch else "@" if is_addr else "=", payload)
28	
29	        if irq_active and not last_irq:
30	            pc = 0x10
31	
32	        if pc >= 0:
33	            if pc in insns:
34	                insn_opcode, insn_desc = insns[pc]
35	                opname = insn_desc.split()[0]
36	
37	                if insn_opcode == 0x0400000b:
38	                    insn_desc = "retirq"
39	                    opname = "retirq"
40	
41	                if is_branch and opname not in ["j", "jal", "jr", "jalr", "ret", "retirq",
42	                        "beq", "bne", "blt", "ble", "bge", "bgt", "bltu", "bleu", "bgeu", "bgtu",
43	                        "beqz", "bnez", "blez", "bgez", "bltz", "bgtz"]:
44	                    print("%s ** UNEXPECTED BRANCH DATA FOR INSN AT %08x! **" % (info, pc))
45	
46	                if is_addr and opname not in ["lb", "lh", "lw", "lbu", "lhu", "sb", "sh", "sw"]:
47	                    print("%s ** UNEXPECTED ADDR DATA FOR INSN AT %08x! **" % (info, pc))
48	
49	                opcode_fmt = "%08x" if (insn_opcode & 3) == 3 else "    %04x"
50	                print(("%s | %08x | " + opcode_fmt + " | %s") % (info, pc, insn_opcode, insn_desc))
51	                if not is_addr:
52	                    pc += 4 if (insn_opcode & 3) == 3 else 2
53	            else:
54	                print("%s ** NO INFORMATION ON INSN AT %08x! **" % (info, pc))
55	                pc = -1
56	        else:
57	            if is_branch:
58	                print("%s ** FOUND BRANCH AND STARTING DECODING **" % info)
59	            else:
60	                print("%s ** SKIPPING DATA UNTIL NEXT BRANCH **" % info)
61	
62	        if is_branch:
63	            pc = payload
64	
65	        last_irq = irq_active
66

Now I have all the information needed. Let me provide the structured summary:

## PicoRV32 Repository - Structural Summary for Learning Handbook

### 1. **firmware/start.S** (536 lines)
**Purpose:** Reset vector, interrupt handler wrapper, test entry points, and hardware mul/div stub functions.

**Main symbols:**
- `reset_vec` - 16-byte reset entry with waitirq/maskirq/jump
- `irq_vec` - Interrupt entry point with full register save/restore
- `start` - Main program entry with register zero-init and test calls
- `hard_mul*`, `hard_div*`, `hard_rem*` - Hardware instruction wrapper functions
- Test entry points: `TEST()` macro for riscv-tests

**Key learning points:**
1. RISC-V ABI register saving conventions (caller-saved vs callee-saved)
2. Custom Q-registers (`q0`-`q3`) for fast interrupt context switching
3. Two interrupt paths: `ENABLE_QREGS` (fast, uses Q-regs) vs standard (uses scratchpad RAM at `0x200`)
4. Reset vector must fit in 16 bytes; jumps to main `start` routine

---

### 2. **firmware/custom_ops.S** (103 lines)
**Purpose:** Assembler macros for PicoRV32's custom IRQ instructions.

**Main macros:**
- `picorv32_getq_insn(rd, qs)` - Read from Q-register
- `picorv32_setq_insn(qd, rs)` - Write to Q-register  
- `picorv32_retirq_insn()` - Return from interrupt
- `picorv32_maskirq_insn(rd, rs)` - Mask interrupt bits
- `picorv32_waitirq_insn(rd)` - Wait for interrupt
- `picorv32_timer_insn(rd, rs)` - Set timer comparator

**Key learning points:**
1. Custom RISC-V instructions use custom opcode `0b0001011` in the R-type format
2. Q-registers (`q0`-`q3`) are hidden registers for interrupt state
3. Bit fields encode operation: funct7 distinguishes between 6 operations
4. Shows how to extend RISC-V ISA with custom instructions

---

### 3. **firmware/irq.c** (140 lines)
**Purpose:** C interrupt handler that processes exceptions and prints diagnostics.

**Main functions:**
- `uint32_t *irq(uint32_t *regs, uint32_t irqs)` - Interrupt handler

**Key learning points:**
1. `regs[0]` contains PC; `regs[1-31]` map to RISC-V registers
2. IRQ bits: bit 0=timer, bit 4=ext_irq_4, bit 5=ext_irq_5, bits 2-3=traps
3. Validates compressed ISA decoding by checking PC LSB matches instruction size
4. Prints full register dump and interrupt statistics on exception

---

### 4. **firmware/firmware.h, firmware/hello.c, firmware/print.c, firmware/sections.lds, firmware/makehex.py**

**firmware.h** (44 lines)
- Function prototypes for print, hello, sieve, multest, stats, irq

**hello.c** (15 lines)
- `void hello(void)` - Prints "hello world\n"

**print.c** (42 lines)
- `print_chr()`, `print_str()`, `print_dec()`, `print_hex()` - Write to UART at `0x10000000`
- **Learning:** Memory-mapped I/O, manual integer-to-string conversion

**sections.lds** (26 lines)
- Defines memory region: 96KB at `0x00000000` for code+data
- **Learning:** Linker script syntax, memory layout control

**makehex.py** (28 lines)
- Converts binary to Verilog hex format (little-endian)
- **Learning:** Binary file processing, endianness handling

---

### 5. **firmware/multest.c, firmware/sieve.c, firmware/stats.c**

**multest.c** (152 lines)
- Tests mul, mulh, mulhsu, mulhu, div, divu, rem, remu instructions
- Compares hardware results vs software computation
- Uses xorshift32 RNG for test vectors

**sieve.c** (85 lines)
- Sieve of Eratosthenes prime algorithm
- Tests memory operations and control flow
- Validates with checksum `0x1772A48F`

**stats.c** (43 lines)
- Uses `rdcycle` and `rdinstret` CSRs
- Computes and prints CPI (cycles per instruction)

**Key learning:** CSR access via inline assembly, benchmarking techniques

---

### 6. **picosoc/README.md** (115 lines)
**Purpose:** Documentation for PicoSoC example SoC.

**Memory map documented:**
- `0x00000000-0x00FFFFFF` - SRAM
- `0x01000000-0x01FFFFFF` - SPI Flash
- `0x02000000-0x0200000B` - Peripheral registers
- `0x03000000+` - User peripherals

**Boot sequence:** Reset vector at `0x00100000` (1MB into flash)

**Learning:** SPI flash modes (SPI/Dual/Quad/DDR/CRM), latency configuration

---

### 7. **picosoc/picosoc.v** (263 lines)
**Purpose:** Top-level SoC module integrating CPU, memory, and peripherals.

**Modules instantiated:**
- `picorv32` - CPU core
- `spimemio` - SPI flash controller
- `simpleuart` - UART
- `picosoc_mem` - SRAM (default 256 words)

**Parameters:** `BARREL_SHIFTER`, `ENABLE_MUL`, `ENABLE_DIV`, `ENABLE_COMPRESSED`, `ENABLE_COUNTERS`, `ENABLE_IRQ_QREGS`, `MEM_WORDS`

**Key signals:** `iomem_*` bus for user peripherals, `irq_*` inputs

**Learning:** Memory-mapped I/O decoding, bus arbitration, parameterized SoC design

---

### 8. **picosoc/spimemio.v** (580 lines)
**Purpose:** SPI flash memory interface controller with QSPI/DDR support.

**Key modules:**
- `spimemio` - Top-level controller with state machine
- `spimemio_xfer` - Low-level SPI bit-banging transfer engine

**Config register bits:** enable, DDR, QSPI, CRM, dummy cycles, bit-bang controls

**Learning:** SPI protocol state machine, configurable transfer modes (normal/dual/quad/DDR)

---

### 9. **picosoc/simpleuart.v** (138 lines)
**Purpose:** Simple UART with clock divider.

**Registers:**
- `0x02000004` - Clock divider
- `0x02000008` - TX data (write), RX data (read)

**Learning:** UART bit-banging state machine, clock divider for baud rate

---

### 10. **picosoc/firmware.c, picosoc/start.s**

**firmware.c** (771 lines)
- Interactive menu system for SPI flash testing
- Functions: `flashio()`, `set_flash_qspi_flag()`, `cmd_benchmark()`, `cmd_memtest()`
- Memory-mapped registers: `reg_spictrl`, `reg_uart_clkdiv`, `reg_uart_data`, `reg_leds`

**start.s** (160 lines)
- Boot code: zero-init registers, clear BSS, copy data, call `main()`
- `flashio_worker_begin/end` - Self-copying SPI bit-bang routine

**Learning:** Self-modifying code, boot sequence stages, SPI flash commands

---

### 11. **picosoc/hx8kdemo.v, picosoc/icebreaker.v**

**hx8kdemo.v** (140 lines)
- FPGA wrapper for Lattice iCE40-HX8K Breakout Board
- 8-bit GPIO mapped to `0x03000000` for LEDs
- Uses `SB_IO` primitives for bidirectional flash pins

**icebreaker.v** (152 lines)
- FPGA wrapper for iCEBreaker board (iCE40 UP5K)
- 5 LEDs + RGB LED mapped
- 32KB SRAM (`MEM_WORDS=32768`)

**Learning:** FPGA I/O primitives, pin constraints (.pcf), reset counter, GPIO memory mapping

---

### 12. **testbench.cc** (45 lines)
**Purpose:** Verilator C++ testbench.

**Features:**
- Clock toggling, reset release after 200 cycles
- VCD tracing (`+vcd`), data bus tracing (`+trace`)
- Detects `trace_valid` signal to output bus transactions

**Learning:** Verilator simulation flow, VCD generation, runtime trace capture

---

### 13. **testbench.v** (480 lines)
**Purpose:** Verilog testbench with AXI4 memory model.

**picorv32_wrapper** module:
- Instantiates `picorv32_axi` CPU and `axi4_memory`
- Loads firmware via `$readmemh` from `+firmware=` argument
- Timer interrupts at periodic intervals
- Trap detection with "ALL TESTS PASSED" on write to `0x20000000`

**axi4_memory** module:
- 128KB memory array
- Optional AXI test mode with randomized delays
- UART output at `0x10000000`

**+args:** `vcd`, `trace`, `verbose`, `firmware=`, `noerror`, `axi_test`

**Learning:** AXI4 protocol, testbench architecture, self-checking tests

---

### 14. **testbench_wb.v** (294 lines)
**Purpose:** Wishbone variant of testbench.

**Differences from AXI version:**
- Uses `picorv32_wb` CPU with Wishbone B4 interface
- `wb_ram` module implements memory-mapped Wishbone slave
- Same firmware loading and trap detection

**Learning:** Wishbone vs AXI protocols, bus adapter patterns

---

### 15. **showtrace.py** (67 lines)
**Purpose:** Disassembles trace output from simulation.

**Features:**
- Parses trace file with encoded PC/branch/addr markers
- Runs `riscv32-unknown-elf-objdump -d` to get instruction disassembly
- Validates that memory access instructions have appropriate trace markers

**Learning:** Trace format decoding, symbol table processing, simulation debugging

---

### 16. **scripts/vivado/**
**Purpose:** Xilinx Vivado synthesis and evaluation scripts.

**Files:**
- `Makefile` - Builds firmware, runs synthesis (`synth_area_*`, `synth_speed`)
- `synth_*.tcl` - Synthesis scripts for different area/speed targets
- `synth_*.v` - Example system for synthesis
- `table.sh`, `tabtest.sh` - Generate area/timing comparison tables

**Learning:** ASIC synthesis flow, area/speed tradeoffs, design evaluation methodology

---

### 17. **scripts/smtbmc/**
**Purpose:** Formal verification with SMT-based model checking.

**Key files:**
- `tracecmp.v/.sh` - Compare trace outputs from formal vs simulation
- `axicheck.v/.sh` - Verify AXI protocol compliance
- `mulcmp.v/.sh` - Verify multiplier correctness
- `.smtc` files - SMT checker configurations

**Learning:** Formal verification with Yosys-SMTBMC, property checking

---

### 18. **scripts/cxxdemo/**
**Purpose:** C++ demo showing firmware compiled with g++.

**Files:**
- `firmware.cc` - C++ test code
- `syscalls.c` - System call stubs
- `start.S`, `start.ld` - Startup and linker script
- `testbench.v` - Testbench
- `Makefile` - Build with riscv-g++

**Learning:** C++ on bare metal, newlib integration, compiler toolchain

---

### 19. **scripts/icestorm/, scripts/yosys/**

**scripts/icestorm/**
- Example LED blinker for iCE40-HX8K
- `example.v`, `example.pcf`, `firmware.c`
- Makefile with synthesis, simulation, P&R targets

**scripts/yosys/**
- `synth_gates.ys` - Yosys synthesis to generic gates
- `synth_sim.ys` - Simulation synthesis
- `synth_osu018.sh` - OSU 0.18um library synthesis

**Learning:** Open-source FPGA flow (Yosys+nextpnr-ice40), library-based synthesis

---

### 20. **picorv32.core, picosoc/*.core files**
**Purpose:** FuseSoC core files for dependency management.

**picorv32.core:**
- Defines filesets: `rtl` (picorv32.v), `tb*` (testbenches)
- Targets: `default`, `lint`, `test`, `test_wb`
- Parameters: `COMPRESSED_ISA`, `axi_test`, `firmware`, `trace`, `vcd`, `verbose`

**picosoc.core:**
- Fileset: `simpleuart.v`, `spimemio.v`, `picosoc.v`
- Parameters: `PICORV32_REGS`, `PICOSOC_MEM`

**hx8kdemo.core / icebreaker.core:**
- Board-specific wrappers with constraints
- Targets: `synth` (icestorm), `sim` (icarus)

**Learning:** Hardware IP packaging, dependency management, build system integration
